title: Uncertainty Sets for Image Classifiers using Conformal Prediction

paragraphs.text:
Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## UNCERTAINTY SETS FOR IMAGE CLASSIFIERS USING CONFORMAL PREDICTION

**Anastasios N. Angelopoulos, Stephen Bates** _[∗][∗]_ **, Jitendra Malik, & Michael I. Jordan** Departments of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences and Statistics University of California, Berkeley

_{_ angelopoulos,stephenbates,malik,jordan _}_ @cs.berkeley.edu

## ABSTRACT

Convolutional image classifiers can achieve high predictive accuracy, but quantifying their uncertainty remains an unresolved challenge, hindering their deployment in consequential settings. Existing uncertainty quantification techniques, such as Platt scaling, attempt to calibrate the network’s probability estimates, but they do not have formal guarantees. We present an algorithm that modifies any classifier to output a _predictive set_ containing the true label with a user-specified probability, such as 90%. The algorithm is simple and fast like Platt scaling, but provides a formal finite-sample coverage guarantee for every model and dataset. Our method modifies an existing conformal prediction algorithm to give more stable predictive sets by regularizing the small scores of unlikely classes after Platt scaling. In experiments on both Imagenet and Imagenet-V2 with ResNet-152 and other classifiers, our scheme outperforms existing approaches, achieving coverage with sets that are often factors of 5 to 10 smaller than a stand-alone Platt scaling baseline.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Imagine you are a doctor making a high-stakes medical decision based on diagnostic information from a computer vision classifier. What would you want the classifier to output in order to make the best decision? This is not a casual hypothetical; such classifiers are already used in medical settings (e.g., Razzak et al., 2018; Lundervold & Lundervold, 2019; Li et al., 2014). A maximumlikelihood diagnosis with an accompanying probability may not be the most essential piece of information. To ensure the health of the patient, you must also rule in or rule out harmful diagnoses. In other words, even if the most likely diagnosis is a stomach ache, it is equally or more important to rule out stomach cancer. Therefore, you would want the classifier to give you—in addition to an estimate of the most likely outcome—actionable uncertainty quantification, such as a set of predictions that provably covers the true diagnosis with a high probability (e.g., 90%). This is called a _prediction set_ (see Figure 1). Our paper describes a method for constructing prediction sets from any pre-trained image classifier that are formally guaranteed to contain the true class with the desired probability, relatively small, and practical to implement. Our method modifies a conformal predictor (Vovk et al., 2005) given in Romano et al. (2020) for the purpose of modern image classification in order to make it more stable in the presence of noisy small probability estimates. Just as importantly, we provide extensive evaluations and code for conformal prediction in computer vision.

Formally, for a discrete response _Y ∈Y_ = _{_ 1 _, . . . , K}_ and a feature vector _X ∈_ R _[d]_ , we desire an uncertainty set function, _C_ ( _X_ ), mapping a feature vector to a subset of _{_ 1 _, . . . , K}_ such that

for a pre-specified confidence level _α_ such as 10%. Conformal predictors like our method can modify any black-box classifier to output predictive sets that are rigorously guaranteed to satisfy the desired _coverage_ property shown in Eq. (1). For evaluations, we focus on Imagenet classification

using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) as the base classifiers, since this is a particularly challenging testbed. In this setting, _X_ would be the image and _Y_ would be the class label. Note that the guarantee in Eq. (1) is _marginal_ over _X_ and _Y_ —it holds on average, not for a particular image _X_ .

A first approach toward this goal might be to assemble the set by including classes from highest to lowest probability (e.g., after Platt scaling and a softmax function; see Platt et al., 1999; Guo et al., 2017) until their sum just exceeds the threshold 1 _− α_ . We call this strategy naive and formulate it precisely in Algorithm 1. There are two problems with naive: first, the probabilities output by CNNs are known to be incorrect (Nixon et al., 2019), so the sets from naive do not achieve coverage. Second, image classification models’ tail probabilities are often badly miscalibrated, leading to large sets that do not faithfully articulate the uncertainty of the model; see Section 2.3. Moreover, smaller sets that achieve the same coverage level can be generated with other methods.

The coverage problem can be solved by picking a new threshold using holdout samples. For example, with _α_ =10%, if choosing sets that contain 93% estimated probability achieves 90% coverage on the holdout set, we use the 93% cutoff instead. We refer to this algorithm, introduced in Romano et al. (2020), as _Adaptive Prediction Sets_ (APS). The APS procedure provides coverage but still produces large sets. To fix this, we introduce a regularization technique that tempers the influence of these noisy estimates, leading to smaller, more stable sets. We describe our proposed algorithm, _Regularized Adaptive Prediction Sets_ (RAPS), in Algorithms 2 and 3 (with APS as a special case). As we will see in Section 2, both APS and RAPS are always guaranteed to satisfy Eq. (1)—regardless of model and dataset. Furthermore, we show that RAPS is guaranteed to have better performance than choosing a fixed-size set. Both methods impose negligible computational requirements in both training and evaluation, and output useful estimates of the model’s uncertainty on a new image given, say, 1000 held-out examples.

In Section 3 we conduct the most extensive evaluation of conformal prediction in deep learning to date on Imagenet and Imagenet-V2. We find that RAPS sets always have smaller average size than naive and APSsets. For example, using a ResNeXt-101, naive does not achieve coverage, while APS and RAPS achieve it almost exactly. However, APS sets have an average size of 19, while RAPS sets have an average size of 2 at _α_ = 10% (Figure 2 and Table 1). We will provide an accompanying codebase that implements our method as a wrapper for any PyTorch classifier, along with code to exactly reproduce all of our experiments.

## 1.1 RELATED WORK

Reliably estimating predictive uncertainty for neural networks is an unsolved problem. Historically, the standard approach has been to train a Bayesian neural network to learn a distribution over network weights (Quinonero-Candela et al., 2005; MacKay, 1992; Neal, 2012; Kuleshov et al., 2018; Gal, 2016). This approach requires computational and algorithmic modifications; other approaches avoid these via ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2018) or approximations of Bayesian inference (Riquelme et al., 2018; Sensoy et al., 2018). These methods also have major practical limitations; for example, ensembling requires training many copies of a neural network adversarially. Therefore, the most widely used strategy is ad-hoc _traditional calibration_ of the softmax scores with Platt scaling (Platt et al., 1999; Guo et al., 2017; Nixon et al., 2019).

This work develops a method for uncertainty quantification based on _conformal prediction_ . Originating in the online learning literature, conformal prediction is an approach for generating predictive sets that satisfy the coverage property in Eq. (1) (Vovk et al., 1999; 2005). We use a convenient data-splitting version known as _split conformal prediction_ that enables conformal prediction meth-

ods to be deployed for essentially any predictor (Papadopoulos et al., 2002; Lei et al., 2018). While mechanically very different from traditional calibration as discussed above, we will refer to our approach as _conformal calibration_ to highlight that the two methodologies have overlapping but different goals.

Conformal prediction is a general framework, not a specific algorithm—important design decisions must be made to achieve the best performance for each context. To this end, Romano et al. (2020) and Cauchois et al. (2020) introduce techniques aimed at achieving coverage that is similar across regions of feature space, whereas Vovk et al. (2003); Hechtlinger et al. (2018) and Guan & Tibshirani (2019) introduce techniques aimed at achieving equal coverage for each class. While these methods have conceptual appeal, thus far there has been limited empirical evaluation of this general approach for state-of-the-art CNNs. Concretely, the only works that we are aware of that include some evaluation of conformal methods on ImageNet—the gold standard for benchmarking computer vision methods—are Hechtlinger et al. (2018), Park et al. (2019), Cauchois et al. (2020), and Messoudi et al. (2020), although in all four cases further experiments are needed to more fully evaluate their operating characteristics for practical deployment. At the heart of conformal prediction is the _conformal score_ - a measure of similarity between labeled examples which is used to compare a new point to among those in a hold out set. Our theoretical contribution can be summarized as a modification of the conformal score from Romano et al. (2020) to have smaller, more stable sets. Lastly, there are alternative approaches to returning prediction sets not based on conformal prediction (Pearce et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). These methods can be used as input to a conformal procedure to potentially improve performance, but they do not have finite-sample coverage guarantees when used alone.

## 2 METHODS

In developing uncertainty set methods to improve upon naive, we are guided by three desiderata. First and most importantly, the _**coverage desideratum**_ says the sets must provide 1 _− α_ coverage, as discussed above. Secondly, the _**size desideratum**_ says we want sets of small size, since these convey more detailed information and may be more useful in practice. Lastly, the _**adaptiveness desideratum**_ says we want the sets to communicate instance-wise uncertainty: they should be smaller for easy test-time examples than for hard ones; see Figure 1 for an illustration. Coverage and size are obviously competing objectives, but size and adaptiveness are also often in tension. The size desideratum seeks small sets, while the adaptiveness desideratum seeks larger sets when the classi-

We now turn to the specifics of our proposed method. We begin in Subsection 2.1 by describing an abstract data-splitting procedure called _conformal calibration_ that enables the near-automatic construction of valid predictive sets (that is, sets satisfying Eq. (1)). Subsequently, in Subsection 2.2, we provide a detailed presentation of our procedure, with commentary in Section 2.3. In Subsection 2.4 we discuss the optimality of our procedure, proving that it is at least as good as the procedure that returns sets of a fixed size, unlike alternative approaches.

## 2.1 CONFORMAL CALIBRATION

We first review a general technique for producing valid prediction sets, following the articulation in Gupta et al. (2019). Consider a procedure that outputs a predictive set for each observation, and further suppose that this procedure has a tuning parameter _τ_ that controls the size of the sets. (In RAPS, _τ_ is the cumulative sum of the sorted, penalized classifier scores.) We take a small independent _conformal calibration set_ of data, and then choose the tuning parameter _τ_ such that the predictive sets are large enough to achieve 1 _− α_ coverage on this set. See Figure 3 for an illustration. This calibration step yields a choice of _τ_ , and the resulting set is formally guaranteed to have coverage 1 _− α_ on a future test point from the same distribution; see Theorem 1 below.

The second argument _u_ is included to allow for randomized procedures; let _U_ 1 _, . . . , Un_ be i.i.d. uniform [0 _,_ 1] random variables that will serve as the second argument for each data point. ## 2.2 OUR METHOD

Conformal calibration is a powerful general idea, allowing one to achieve the coverage desideratum for any choice of sets _C_ ( _x, u, τ_ ). Nonetheless, this is not yet a full solution, since the quality of the resulting prediction sets can vary dramatically depending on the design of _C_ ( _x, u, τ_ ). In particular, we recall the size and adaptiveness desiderata from Section 1—we want our uncertainty sets to be as small as possible while faithfully articulating the instance-wise uncertainty of each test point. In this section, we explicitly give our algorithm, which can be viewed as a special case of conformal calibration with the uncertainty sets _C_ designed to extract information from CNNs.

Our algorithm has three main ingredients. First, for a feature vector _x_ , the base model computes class probabilities _π_ ˆ _x ∈_ R _[k]_ , and we order the classes from most probable to least probable. Then, we add a regularization term to promote small predictive sets. Finally, we conformally calibrate the penalized prediction sets to guarantee coverage on future test points.

2.3 WHY REGULARIZE?

In our experiments, the sets from APS are larger than necessary, because APS is sensitive to the noisy probability estimates far down the list of classes. This noise leads to a _permutation problem_ of unlikely classes, where ordering of the classes with small probability estimates is determined mostly by random chance. If 5% of the true classes from the calibration set are deep in the tail due to the permutation problem, APS will choose large 95% predictive sets; see Figure 2. The inclusion of the RAPS regularization causes the algorithm to avoid using the unreliable probabilities in the tail; see Figure 4. We discuss how RAPS improves the adaptiveness of APS in Section 4 and Appendix E.

2.4 OPTIMALITY CONSIDERATIONS

(Section 3.5 shows the parameters are easy to select and RAPS is not sensitive to their values). Table 1: **Results on Imagenet-Val.** We report coverage and size of the optimal, randomized fixed sets, naive, APS, and RAPS sets for nine different Imagenet classifiers. The median-of-means for each column is reported over 100 different trials. See Section 3.1 for full details.

In this section we report on experiments that study the performance of the predictive sets from naive, APS, and RAPS, evaluating each based on the three desiderata above. We begin with a brief preview of the experiments. In **Experiment 1** , we evaluate naive, APS, and RAPS on ImagenetVal. Both APS and RAPS provided almost exact coverage, while naive sets had coverage slightly below the specified level. APS has larger sets on average than naive and RAPS. RAPS has a much smaller average set size than APS and naive. In **Experiment 2** , we repeat Experiment 1 on Imagenet-V2, and the conclusions still hold. In **Experiment 3** , we produce histograms of set sizes for naive, APS, and RAPS for several different values of _λ_ , illustrating a simple tradeoff between set size and adaptiveness. In **Experiment 4** , we compute histograms of RAPS sets stratified by image difficulty, showing that RAPS sets are smaller for easier images than for difficult ones. In **Experiment 5** , we report the performance of RAPS with many values of the tuning parameters.

In our experiments, we use nine standard, pretrained Imagenet classifiers from the torchvision repository (Paszke et al., 2019) with standard normalization, resize, and crop parameters. Before applying naive, APS, or RAPS, we calibrated the classifiers using the standard temperature scaling/Platt scaling procedure as in Guo et al. (2017) on the calibration set. Thereafter, naive, APS, and RAPS were applied, with RAPS using a data-driven choice of parameters described in Appendix E. We use the randomized versions of these algorithms—see Appendix B for a discussion.

3.1 EXPERIMENT 1: COVERAGE VS SET SIZE ON IMAGENET

In this experiment, we calculated the coverage and mean set size of each procedure for two different choices of _α_ . Over 100 trials, we randomly sampled two subsets of Imagenet-Val: one conformal calibration subset of size 20K and one evaluation subset of size 20K. The median-of-means over trials for both coverage and set size are reported in Table 1. Figure 2 illustrates the performances of naive, APS, and RAPS; RAPS has much smaller sets than both naive and APS, while achieving coverage. We also report results from a conformalized fixed-k procedure, which finds the smallest fixed set size achieving coverage on the holdout set, _k[∗]_ , then predicts sets of size _k[∗] −_ 1 or _k[∗]_ on new examples in order to achieve exact coverage; see Algorithm 4 in Appendix E.

3.2 EXPERIMENT 2: COVERAGE VS SET SIZE ON IMAGENET-V2

The same procedure as Experiment 1 was repeated on Imagenet-V2, with exactly the same normalization, resize, and crop parameters. The size of the calibration and evaluation sets was 5K, since Imagenet-V2 is a smaller dataset. The result shows that our method can still provide coverage even for models trained on different distributions, as long as the conformal calibration set comes from the new distribution. The variance of the coverage is higher due to having less data.

3.3 EXPERIMENT 3: SET SIZES OF N A I V E, APS, AND RAPS ON IMAGENET

We investigate the effect of regularization in more detail. For three values of _λ_ , we collected the set sizes produced by each of naive, APS, and RAPS and report their histograms in Figure 4.

3.4 EXPERIMENT 4: ADAPTIVENESS OF RAPS ON IMAGENET

Experiments 3 and 4 together illustrate the tradeoff between adaptiveness and size: as the average set size decreases, the RAPS procedure truncates sets larger than the smallest fixed set that provides coverage, taming the heavy tail of the APS procedure. Since RAPS with large _λ_ undercovers hard examples, it must compensate by taking larger sets for easy examples to ensure the 1 _− α_ marginal coverage guarantee. However, the size only increases slightly since easy images are more common than hard ones, and the total probability mass can often exceed _τ_ ˆ _ccal_ by including only one more class. If this behavior is not desired, we can instead automatically pick _λ_ to optimize the adaptiveness of RAPS; see Section 4.

## 3.5 EXPERIMENT 5: CHOICE OF TUNING PARAMETERS

While any value of the tuning parameters _λ_ and _kreg_ lead to coverage (Proposition 1), some values will lead to smaller sets. In Experiments 1 and 2, we chose _kreg_ and _λ_ adaptively from data (see Appendix E), achieving strong results for all models and choices of the coverage level. Table 3 gives the performance of RAPS with many choices of _kreg_ and _λ_ for ResNet-152.

## 4 ADAPTIVENESS AND CONDITIONAL COVERAGE

In this section, we point to a definition of adaptiveness that is more natural for the image classification setting than the existing notion of conditional coverage. We show that APS does not satisfy conditional coverage, and that RAPS with small _λ_ outperforms it in terms of adaptiveness.

We say that a set-valued predictor _C_ : R _[d] →_ 2 _[Y]_ satisfies exact _conditional coverage_ if _P_ ( _Y ∈ C_ ( _X_ ) _| X_ = _x_ ) = 1 _− α_ for each _x_ . Distribution-free guarantees on conditional coverage are impossible (Vovk, 2012; Lei & Wasserman, 2014), but many algorithms try to satisfy it approximately (Romano et al., 2019; 2020; Cauchois et al., 2020). In a similar spirit, Tibshirani et al. (2019) suggest a notion of local conditional coverage, where one asks for coverage in a neighborhood of each point, weighted according to a chosen kernel. Cauchois et al. (2020) introduce the _worst-case slab_ metric for measuring violations of the conditional coverage property. We present a different way of measuring violations of conditional coverage.

In words, if conditional coverage holds, then coverage holds after stratifying by set size. Based on this result, In Appendix E, we introduce the _size-stratified coverage violation_ criterion, a simple and pragmatic way of quantifying adaptiveness. Then, we automatically tune _λ_ on this metric so RAPS markedly outperforms the adaptiveness of APS (see Table 8).

Table 4: **Coverage conditional on set size.** We report average coverage of images stratified by the size of the set output by RAPS using a ResNet-152 for varying _λ_ . The marginal coverage rate is 90%.

In Table 4, we report on the coverage of APS and RAPS, stratified by the size of the prediction set. Turning our attention to the _λ_ = 0 column, we see that when APS outputs a set of size 101 _−_ 1000, APS has coverage 97%, substantially higher than 90% nominal rate. By Proposition 3, we conclude that APS is not achieving exact conditional coverage, because the scores are far from the oracle probabilities. The APS procedure still achieves marginal coverage by overcovering hard examples and undercovering easy ones, an undesirable behavior. Alternatively, RAPS can be used to regularize the set sizes—for _λ_ = _._ 001 to _λ_ = _._ 01 the coverage stratified by set size is more balanced. In summary, even purely based on the adaptiveness desideratum, RAPS with light regularization is preferable to APS. Note that as the size of the training data increases, as long as _π_ ˆ is consistent, naive and APS will become more stable, and so we expect less regularization will be needed.

Lastly, we argue that conditional coverage is a poor notion of adaptiveness when the best possible model (i.e., one fit on infinite data) has high accuracy. Given such a model, the oracle procedure from Romano et al. (2020) would return the correct label with probability 1 _− α_ and the empty set with probability _α_ . That is, having correct conditional coverage for high-signal problems where _Y_ is perfectly determined by _X_ requires a perfect classifier. In our experiments on ImageNet, APS does not approximate this behavior. Therefore, conditional coverage isn’t the right goal for prediction sets with realistic sample sizes. Proposition 3 suggests a relaxation. We could require that we have the right coverage, no matter the size of the prediction set: _P_ ( _Y ∈C_ ( _X_ ) _| {|C_ ( _x_ ) _| ∈A}_ ) _≥_ 1 _− α_ for any _A ⊂{_ 0 _,_ 1 _,_ 2 _, . . . }_ ; Appendix E.2 develops this idea. We view this as a promising way to reason about adaptiveness in high-signal problems such as image classification.

## 5 DISCUSSION

For classification tasks with many possible labels, our method enables a researcher to take any base classifier and return predictive sets guaranteed to achieve a pre-specified error level, such as 90%, while retaining small average size. It is simple to deploy, so it is an attractive, automatic way to quantify the uncertainty of image classifiers—an essential task in such settings as medical diagnostics, self-driving vehicles, and flagging dangerous internet content. Predictive sets in computer vision (from RAPS and other conformal methods) have many further uses, since they systematically identify hard test-time examples. Finding such examples is useful in active learning where one only has resources to label a small number of points. In a different direction, one can improve efficiency of a classifier by using a cheap classifier outputting a prediction set first, and an expensive one only when the cheap classifier outputs a large set (a _cascade_ ; see, e.g., Li et al. 2015), and Fisch et al. (2021) for an implementation of conformal prediction in this setting. One can also use predictive sets during model development to identify failure cases and outliers and suggest strategies for improving its performance. Prediction sets are most useful for problems with many classes; returning to our initial medical motivation, we envision RAPS could be used by a doctor to automatically screen for a large number of diseases (e.g. via a blood sample) and refer the patient to relevant specialists.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a well-motivated and timely method—RAPS—for constructing uncertainty sets in image classification with formal coverage guarantees. It builds on solid foundations in conformal prediction and improves upon existing methods like APS by introducing regularization to stabilize tail probabilities. The empirical evaluation is extensive across multiple models and datasets, and the method achieves impressive reductions in average set size while maintaining coverage. The work fills an important gap in reliable uncertainty quantification for high-stakes applications.

Overview issues:

- Overstatement of adaptiveness claims without conditional coverage: The paper positions RAPS as more 'adaptive' than APS by showing that set sizes vary with image difficulty and that size-stratified coverage is more balanced (Table 4). However, this is framed as progress toward conditional coverage, which the paper correctly notes is unattainable under distribution-free assumptions. While the size-stratified coverage criterion is a pragmatic proxy, the paper does not sufficiently acknowledge that marginal coverage alone cannot ensure meaningful instance-wise uncertainty. Readers may be misled into thinking RAPS provides stronger guarantees than it does—particularly in settings like medical diagnosis where conditional reliability matters. The claim that RAPS 'outperforms APS in adaptiveness' needs qualification: it trades off adaptiveness for smaller average size via regularization, not improved conditional calibration.

- Insufficient discussion of failure modes under dataset shift: Although the paper evaluates RAPS on ImageNet-V2, a distributionally shifted version of ImageNet, it does not probe the limits of coverage under more severe shifts. The guarantee in Theorem 1 relies on exchangeability between calibration and test data, which fails under covariate or concept shift. The paper states that 'our method can still provide coverage' on ImageNet-V2, but Table 2 shows that naive fails while RAPS maintains coverage—this is expected because conformal methods are robust to mild shifts as long as the calibration set comes from the test distribution. However, no experiments consider cases where the calibration set is outdated or biased, which would break the guarantee. A critical limitation for real-world deployment is thus underexplored.

- Regularization mechanism conflates model uncertainty with rank noise: The core innovation of RAPS is the regularization term $\lambda \cdot (o_x(y) - k_{\text{reg}})^+$, which penalizes inclusion of low-ranked classes. The justification is that tail probabilities are noisy and lead to unstable rankings (Section 2.3). However, the method does not distinguish between genuine but small probabilities and estimation noise. By penalizing based solely on rank position, RAPS risks suppressing rare but correct classes—especially in long-tailed datasets where some classes are inherently unlikely but still possible. This could lead to systematic under-coverage for rare classes, even if marginal coverage is preserved. The paper does not evaluate per-class coverage or failure rates, making it impossible to assess this risk. A more principled approach might model tail uncertainty explicitly rather than truncating by rank.

- Lack of comparison to alternative conformal scoring rules: The paper compares RAPS only to naive and APS, both of which use cumulative probability as the scoring mechanism. However, other conformal methods like class-conditional calibration (Guan & Tibshirani, 2019) or localized scores could achieve better conditional behavior or robustness. By focusing exclusively on improving APS via regularization, the paper misses an opportunity to position RAPS within a broader design space of conformity scores. For example, a method that adaptively weights classes by frequency or difficulty might outperform fixed-rank regularization. Without such comparisons, the superiority of RAPS appears relative rather than absolute, limiting the paper's impact on future method design.

- Ambiguity in the optimality claim of Proposition 2: Proposition 2 states that RAPS 'dominates' top-k sets by producing smaller or equal sets while maintaining coverage. However, the result requires $k_{\text{reg}} \leq k^*$ and $\lambda = 1$, which forces RAPS to regularize heavily—essentially reverting to a top-$k^*$ predictor. In practice, the paper uses much smaller $\lambda$ values (e.g., 0.01 in Table 3), where the proposition no longer applies. This creates a tension: the theoretical 'optimality' holds only in a regime where RAPS loses its adaptiveness, while the practical success of RAPS comes from using small $\lambda$. The paper should clarify that the proposition illustrates a safety property (RAPS won’t be worse than top-k), not a performance guarantee under typical settings.

- No evaluation of rare class coverage under RAPS: The paper claims RAPS produces smaller, more stable prediction sets by penalizing low-ranked classes, but it does not evaluate whether this harms coverage for rare or tail classes. Since the regularization is rank-based, not probability-based, it may systematically exclude infrequent but correct labels, especially in long-tailed distributions like ImageNet. While marginal coverage is preserved by construction, per-class coverage could be uneven—a critical issue in medical or safety applications where missing a rare diagnosis has high cost. To verify the method’s robustness, the authors should compute and report coverage rates stratified by ground-truth class frequency, using the ImageNet class hierarchy or frequency bins. This would show whether RAPS maintains fairness across common and rare classes.

- Missing ablation on regularization design choices: The RAPS method introduces an ad-hoc penalty based on class rank, but no alternative regularization schemes are tested. For example, a probability-mass-based penalty or a learned weighting of tail classes might perform better. The paper would be strengthened by a direct comparison of different regularization forms—such as penalizing cumulative mass beyond a threshold, or using class frequency as a prior—under the same conformal framework. Without this, it is unclear whether the chosen form is principled or merely heuristic. Adding a small ablation in Table 3 or a new figure showing set size and coverage for different penalty types would clarify the design rationale and improve reproducibility.

- No guidance on selecting kreg and λ in practice: Although Appendix E mentions a data-driven selection for λ and kreg, the paper does not specify how these parameters should be chosen when computational budget is limited or when no held-out validation beyond the calibration set is available. Practitioners need clear heuristics—e.g., setting kreg near the median top-k size of APS, or cross-validating λ on a subset of the calibration data. The current presentation treats parameter selection as an implementation detail, but it is central to achieving the reported performance. The authors should add a short subsection or empirical rule in Section 3.5, backed by results from Table 3, to guide users in tuning RAPS without overfitting the calibration set.

- Lack of comparison to class-conditional conformal methods: The paper positions RAPS as an improvement over APS and naive baselines, but does not compare to other conformal approaches that address similar instability issues, such as class-conditional calibration (Guan & Tibshirani, 2019) or difficulty-based scoring. These methods also aim to stabilize prediction sets and could offer better conditional coverage or robustness to class imbalance. A direct comparison on the same models and datasets would clarify whether rank regularization is superior to alternative strategies. At minimum, the authors should evaluate one such method on ImageNet-Val and include it in Table 1 to contextualize RAPS’s gains beyond the APS baseline.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Clarify the limitations of the adaptiveness claims, distinguishing between marginal coverage and conditional reliability, and avoid implying that size-stratified coverage implies better instance-wise uncertainty quantification.

- Add analysis of per-class or per-group coverage, particularly for rare or hard classes, to evaluate whether regularization introduces systematic biases.

- Expand discussion of failure modes under dataset shift, including a scenario where calibration and test data are not exchangeable (e.g., temporal drift or domain mismatch).

- Include comparisons to alternative conformal scoring strategies (e.g., class-conditional or localized scores) to better position RAPS within the space of uncertainty quantification methods.

- Revise the interpretation of Proposition 2 to emphasize it is a worst-case safeguard, not a description of typical performance, and align the theoretical and empirical parameter regimes.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about naive set size on Inception
comments.explanation: The reported average set size of 75.4 for the naive method on Inception contradicts expected behavior. Given the model's top-5 accuracy of 88.7%, the naive method—based on cumulative softmax probabilities—should produce sets of size close to 5, not 75. This suggests either a misimplementation (e.g., inverted scores or incorrect thresholding) or a reporting error. Other models show naive set sizes between 10–17, making Inception’s value an outlier. If accurate, the paper must explain this anomaly; otherwise, the value should be corrected.

comments.title: Misleading average set size claim for RAPS
comments.quote: For example, using a ResNeXt-101, naive does not achieve coverage, while APS and RAPS achieve it almost exactly. However, APS sets have an average size of 19, while RAPS sets have an average size of 2 at  $\alpha = 10\%$  (Figure 2 and Table 1).
comments.explanation: The claim that RAPS achieves an average set size of 2 on ResNeXt-101 at $\alpha = 10\%$ is inconsistent with Table 1, which reports a value of 2.00 for RAPS but 19.7 for APS. A set size of 2 implies near-perfect confidence in only two classes across ImageNet’s 1000 classes, which is implausible for a model with 79.3% top-1 accuracy. While the number appears in the table, the surrounding text presents it as a typical outcome without acknowledging that such small sets may arise from specific data splits or parameter choices. The discussion should clarify whether this reflects median behavior or an optimistic case.

comments.title: Circular definition in RAPS prediction algorithm
comments.quote: L ← |j ∈ Y : Σj=0l s i + λ(L - kreg) + ≤ τccal | + 1
comments.explanation: The expression in Algorithm 3, line 2, defines $L$ using a condition that depends on $L$ itself through $\lambda(L - k_{\text{reg}})^+$. This creates a circular dependency: the value of $L$ cannot be computed without knowing $L$. For instance, whether a class at position $l=6$ is included depends on whether $L > k_{\text{reg}}$, but $L$ is the output of the cardinality computation. The correct implementation should compute cumulative scores $S_l = \sum_{j=1}^l s_{i,j} + \lambda(l - k_{\text{reg}})^+$ for $l = 1,\dots,K$, then find the largest $l$ such that $S_l \leq \tau_{\text{ccal}}$. The current form is mathematically ill-defined and unimplementable.

comments.title: Inconsistent summation bounds in calibration and prediction
comments.quote: Ei← Σj=0Li s i,j + λ(Li - kreg + 1)+
comments.explanation: The summation $\sum_{j=0}^{L_i}$ in Algorithm 2, line 4, includes index $j=0$, which is out of bounds for standard 1-based indexing of sorted scores. If $s_{i,j}$ represents the $j$-th highest probability, $j$ should range from 1 to $K$. Including $j=0$ introduces ambiguity—either an undefined $s_{i,0}$ or a zero-padding assumption. Moreover, the sum should include the true class’s probability to align with the test-time score. The correct form is $\sum_{j=1}^{L_i} s_{i,j}$, ensuring proper indexing and consistency with the definition of $\rho_x(y) + \hat{\pi}_x(y)$. This indexing error risks off-by-one bugs in implementation.

comments.title: Regularization term offset error in calibration
comments.quote: Ei← Σj=0Li s i,j + λ(Li - kreg + 1)+
comments.explanation: The calibration score in Algorithm 2 uses $\lambda(L_i - k_{\text{reg}} + 1)^+$, but Eq. (4) defines the regularization term as $\lambda(o_x(y) - k_{\text{reg}})^+$. Since $o_x(y) = L_i$, this corresponds to $\lambda(L_i - k_{\text{reg}})^+$. The +1 offset means that when $L_i = k_{\text{reg}}$, the algorithm applies a penalty $\lambda$, while at test time the same label incurs no penalty. This over-penalizes the true label during calibration, inflating $\hat{\tau}_{\text{ccal}}$ and leading to larger prediction sets than intended. The error breaks consistency between calibration and test scoring. Line 4 should be corrected to `Ei ← Σ_{j=1}^{L_i} s_{i,j} + λ(Li - kreg)+`.

comments.title: Incorrect theoretical containment claim in Proposition 2
comments.quote:
al {C} ^ {*} \left(X _ {n + 1}, U _ {n + 1}, \hat {\tau} _ {\text {c c a l}}\right) \subseteq \left\{\hat {y} _ {(1)} (x), \dots , \hat {y} _ {(k ^ {*})} (x) \right\}.
$$

In wo
comments.explanation: The claimed set containment is not valid. The right-hand side uses a free variable $x$, while the left-hand side depends on $X_{n+1}$, creating a type mismatch. Even if interpreted pointwise, RAPS modifies class ordering via regularization: a class outside the top-$k^*$ may enter $\mathcal{C}^*$ due to low penalty, while a top-$k^*$ class may be excluded due to high penalty. Thus, neither inclusion holds in general. The intended claim is likely about expected size: $\mathbb{E}[|\mathcal{C}^*|] \leq k^*$. This would require proof under $\lambda = 1$ and $k_{\text{reg}} \leq k^*$, but it is not implied by set containment. The proposition should be revised to reflect an average size bound.

comments.title: Theoretical result applies only in non-adaptive regime
comments.quote: Take  $\mathcal{C}^* (x,u,\tau)$  as in Eq. (4) with any  $k_{reg}\leq k^{*}$  and  $\lambda = 1$ . Then with  $\hat{\tau}_{\mathrm{ccal}}$  chosen as in Eq. (3), we have
comments.explanation: The proposition requires $\lambda = 1$, which imposes full regularization penalty on classes beyond $k_{\text{reg}}$, effectively capping set size at $k_{\text{reg}}$. This reduces RAPS to a nearly fixed-size predictor, contradicting its design goal of adaptiveness. The experiments use much smaller $\lambda$ values (e.g., 0.01), where regularization is mild and sets remain adaptive. Thus, the 'dominance' over top-$k$ sets is established in a regime where RAPS loses its key advantage. The paper should clarify that this is a safety property under heavy regularization, not a performance guarantee under typical settings.

comments.title: Misleading interpretation of size-stratified coverage
comments.quote: By Proposition 3, we conclude that APS is not achieving exact conditional coverage, because the scores are far from the oracle probabilities.
comments.explanation: This misapplies Proposition 3. The proposition states that exact conditional coverage implies size-stratified coverage equals $1-\alpha$, so deviation from $1-\alpha$ violates a necessary condition. However, attributing this failure to 'scores being far from oracle probabilities' overreaches—the proposition makes no claim about score quality. A more accurate statement is that the variation in size-stratified coverage (e.g., 97% for large sets) proves that exact conditional coverage does not hold. The diagnostic claim about score fidelity is unsupported by the result and should be removed.

comments.title: Incorrect oracle behavior in high-accuracy setting
comments.quote: Given such a model, the oracle procedure from Romano et al. (2020) would return the correct label with probability  $1 - \alpha$  and the empty set with probability  $\alpha$ .
comments.explanation: This mischaracterizes the oracle in Romano et al. (2020). The oracle does not return the empty set with probability $\alpha$—that would violate coverage. Instead, it returns the singleton set containing the true label with probability $1-\alpha$ and a larger set (containing the true label) with probability $\alpha$, ensuring coverage while minimizing size. The argument against conditional coverage should focus on the instability of such extreme set sizes in practice, not on a flawed description of the oracle. Correcting this strengthens the paper’s conceptual framing.

comments.title: Overstated practical utility in high-stakes domains
comments.quote: It is simple to deploy, so it is an attractive, automatic way to quantify the uncertainty of image classifiers—an essential task in such settings as medical diagnostics, self-driving vehicles, and flagging dangerous internet content.
comments.explanation: The claim that the method is 'an attractive, automatic way' to quantify uncertainty in high-stakes domains overreaches its guarantees. It provides only marginal coverage, not conditional coverage, so it does not ensure reliable uncertainty for individual inputs. In medical settings, missing a rare diagnosis due to rank-based regularization could have severe consequences. The discussion does not acknowledge this limitation when motivating applications. The sentence should be revised to clarify that the method ensures average coverage but may not capture instance-wise uncertainty adequately for all use cases.

comments.title: Ambiguous conditioning notation in adaptiveness criterion
comments.quote: P(Y \in \mathcal{C}(X) \mid \{|C(x)| \in \mathcal{A}\})
comments.explanation: The notation $\{|C(x)| \in \mathcal{A}\}$ is inconsistent and ambiguous. It uses lowercase $c$ and $x$, while $\mathcal{C}(X)$ is the random set-valued function. This creates confusion between realizations and random variables. The conditioning event should be written as $\{ |\mathcal{C}(X)| \in \mathcal{A} \}$ to clearly indicate the cardinality of the random set. Standardizing to uppercase script $\mathcal{C}$ and random variable $X$ improves clarity and aligns with standard probability notation.

comments.title: Incorrect relaxation of conditional coverage requirement
comments.quote: We could require that we have the right coverage, no matter the size of the prediction set:  $P(Y \in \mathcal{C}(X) \mid \{|C(x)| \in \mathcal{A}\}) \geq 1 - \alpha$  for any  $\mathcal{A} \subset \{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ ; Appendix E.2 develops this idea.
comments.explanation: The proposed relaxation requires coverage to be at least $1-\alpha$ within each size stratum, but this allows for overcoverage and does not ensure adaptiveness. A trivial predictor that always returns the full label set satisfies this condition. For adaptiveness, one wants coverage close to $1-\alpha$ across strata. The correct relaxation—implied by Proposition 3—is equality: $P(Y \in \mathcal{C}(X) \mid \{ |\mathcal{C}(X)| \in \mathcal{A} \}) = 1 - \alpha$. The $\geq$ formulation undermines the goal of balanced, instance-adaptive prediction and should be corrected.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Regularized Adaptive Prediction Sets (RAPS), a conformal prediction method for generating uncertainty sets in image classification. While the method achieves strong empirical results with smaller set sizes than baselines while maintaining coverage guarantees, there are significant concerns about the exchangeability assumption, limited analysis of conditional coverage, and insufficient exploration of the method's limitations under distribution shift.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by adapting conformal prediction to modern image classification with a practical regularization approach. The empirical evaluation on ImageNet is comprehensive, and the method's ability to produce smaller sets while maintaining formal coverage is impressive. However, the paper glosses over important assumptions about data exchangeability and provides limited analysis of how the method performs under realistic distribution shifts.

Overview issues:

- Exchangeability assumption not empirically validated: The paper's coverage guarantees (Theorem 1, Proposition 1) rely critically on the assumption that calibration and test data are exchangeable. While this is stated as a technical remark in Section 2.1, the paper provides no empirical verification of this assumption. ImageNet validation sets may not be exchangeable with real-world deployment data due to dataset collection biases, temporal shifts, or domain changes. The ImageNet-V2 experiment (Section 3.2) hints at this issue by showing higher coverage variance, but the paper doesn't analyze whether the exchangeability assumption holds or quantify its violation. This is particularly concerning given the medical diagnosis motivation in the introduction, where distribution shift between training/deployment environments is common.

- Conditional coverage analysis is insufficient: Section 4 discusses adaptiveness and conditional coverage, but the analysis is incomplete. While Proposition 3 provides a theoretical connection between conditional coverage and size-stratified coverage, the paper only reports size-stratified coverage for RAPS (Table 4) without showing comparable results for APS. The claim that APS 'does not satisfy conditional coverage' is not empirically demonstrated through standard conditional coverage metrics (e.g., coverage conditioned on confidence levels or image features). The proposed size-stratified coverage violation criterion is mentioned but not defined in the main text, making it difficult to evaluate the claimed adaptiveness improvements. For a method claiming to improve adaptiveness, more rigorous conditional coverage analysis across different subgroups (e.g., by image difficulty, class, or semantic features) is needed.

- Limited exploration of distribution shift robustness: The paper evaluates on ImageNet-V2 but doesn't systematically study performance under various types of distribution shift that are common in practice. The literature context identifies non-IID data and domain shift as open challenges, yet the paper only tests on one shifted dataset (ImageNet-V2). There's no analysis of how calibration set size affects robustness to shift, or how the method performs under more severe corruptions (e.g., ImageNet-C). The claim in Section 3.2 that the method 'can still provide coverage even for models trained on different distributions' is too strong - it only holds when the calibration set matches the test distribution, which may not be realistic in deployment scenarios where calibration data may be limited or collected from a different source.

- Scalability analysis is missing for large label spaces: While the paper demonstrates results on ImageNet (1000 classes), it doesn't address scalability to much larger label spaces that are becoming common in modern vision systems. The literature context identifies this as an open challenge, but the paper provides no theoretical or empirical analysis of how RAPS scales with the number of classes. The computational complexity of sorting probabilities and applying regularization grows with K, and the statistical efficiency may degrade in extreme multi-class settings. For a method positioned as practical for deployment, understanding its behavior with 10,000+ classes (as in some hierarchical classification or retrieval systems) is important.

- Calibration set size sensitivity is not thoroughly studied: The paper uses 20K calibration samples for ImageNet and 5K for ImageNet-V2, but doesn't analyze how sensitive the method is to calibration set size. This is crucial for practical deployment where calibration data may be limited. Section 3.5 shows parameter sensitivity but not calibration size sensitivity. The conformal prediction framework's finite-sample guarantees depend on having enough calibration data, and the regularization parameters might need adjustment for smaller calibration sets. Without this analysis, it's unclear how well RAPS would work in low-data regimes like medical diagnosis, where collecting large calibration sets may be expensive or infeasible.

- Missing demonstration of regularization necessity: The paper claims that regularization is needed to handle noisy tail probabilities, but provides no concrete example showing when and why APS fails without regularization. A simple synthetic example with a known probability distribution could illustrate how small perturbations in tail probabilities lead to large set size inflation in APS. For instance, consider a 10-class problem where the true probabilities are [0.4, 0.3, 0.15, 0.05, 0.04, 0.03, 0.02, 0.005, 0.005, 0] but the estimated probabilities have small errors in the tail. Showing how APS's set size changes dramatically while RAPS remains stable would make the motivation for regularization concrete.

- No worked parametric example: Despite being a methods paper, RAPS is never demonstrated on a simple, fully-specified model where all quantities can be computed analytically. A standard example would be a multinomial logistic regression with known parameters on synthetic data, where one could compute the exact conformal scores and verify that the coverage bounds hold. This would help readers understand how the regularization term interacts with the conformal calibration step in a controlled setting, and verify that the theoretical results match practice.

- Implementation guidance for hyperparameter selection: While Appendix E mentions data-driven selection of λ and k_reg, the main text provides no practical guidance for choosing these parameters. The paper should include a concrete algorithm or heuristic for parameter selection, such as cross-validation on the calibration set or an analytical formula based on calibration set size. Without this, practitioners cannot easily deploy the method, especially in domains where calibration data is limited. A simple rule-of-thumb like 'set k_reg to the 5th percentile of calibration set ranks and λ to achieve target average set size' would make the method more accessible.

- Conditional coverage analysis incomplete: Section 4 proposes size-stratified coverage as a metric for adaptiveness but never defines it formally or provides a complete analysis. The paper should define the size-stratified coverage violation criterion mathematically and show how RAPS optimizes it. More importantly, it should include comparative results showing that RAPS achieves better conditional coverage than APS across multiple conditioning variables (confidence levels, image difficulty metrics, or semantic features). Without this, the claim that RAPS improves adaptiveness remains unsubstantiated.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add empirical validation of the exchangeability assumption on calibration vs test data, including metrics to quantify violations

- Provide comprehensive conditional coverage analysis using standard metrics and compare APS vs RAPS across different subgroups

- Include experiments on additional distribution shift scenarios (e.g., ImageNet-C) and analyze performance when calibration/test distributions differ

- Add scalability analysis for larger label spaces with both theoretical complexity and empirical results

- Study sensitivity to calibration set size and provide guidelines for parameter selection in low-data regimes

comments.title: Overstated comparison claim in abstract
comments.quote: In experiments on both Imagenet and Imagenet-V2 with ResNet-152 and other classifiers, our scheme outperforms existing approaches, achieving coverage with sets that are often factors of 5 to 10 smaller than a stand-alone Platt scaling baseline.
comments.explanation: The claim of 5-10x reduction in set size compared to Platt scaling appears exaggerated based on the paper's own results. Table 1 in Section 3.1 shows RAPS achieving average set sizes of 1.59-2.10 for ResNet-152 at 90% coverage, while Platt scaling achieves 5.40-7.60. This represents a 2.4-4.8x reduction, not 5-10x. The abstract should reflect the actual empirical results shown in the paper. Rewrite the final sentence as 'achieving coverage with sets that are 2.4 to 4.8 times smaller than a stand-alone Platt scaling baseline' to match the reported experimental results.

comments.title: Inaccurate characterization of ensembling limitations
comments.quote: These methods also have major practical limitations; for example, ensembling requires training many copies of a neural network adversarially.
comments.explanation: The claim that ensembling requires training neural networks 'adversarially' is incorrect. Standard ensembling methods like Deep Ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017) train networks independently with different random initializations and data shuffling, not adversarially. Adversarial training refers to training against adversarial examples, which is a different concept. The paper should correct this to accurately state that ensembling requires training multiple models independently, which increases computational cost but does not involve adversarial training.

comments.title: Permutation problem claim lacks justification
comments.quote: This noise leads to a permutation problem of unlikely classes, where ordering of the classes with small probability estimates is determined mostly by random chance. If  $5\%$  of the true classes from the calibration set are deep in the tail due to the permutation problem, APS will choose large  $95\%$  predictive sets; see Figure 2.
comments.explanation: The claim that 5% of true classes being in the tail causes APS to produce 95% predictive sets is not mathematically justified. APS selects the threshold τ based on the (1-α) quantile of conformity scores on the calibration set. The presence of some true classes in the tail affects the conformity scores for those specific calibration points, but does not directly determine the final set size. The relationship between tail class frequency and resulting set size depends on the distribution of conformity scores, not just the percentage of tail classes. The authors should provide a derivation or simulation showing how 5% tail classes leads to 95% set sizes, or remove this specific quantitative claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent coverage values in Table 1
comments.explanation: The table shows inconsistent coverage values that contradict the paper's claims. For VGG16, APS coverage is 0.901 and RAPS coverage is 0.900, but the text states 'Both APS and RAPS provided almost exact coverage'. More critically, for Inception, APS coverage is 0.900 while RAPS coverage is 0.901, and for VGG16, APS coverage exceeds RAPS (0.901 vs 0.900). These inconsistencies contradict the claim that RAPS consistently achieves the target coverage level of 0.900. The table should be corrected to show that both APS and RAPS achieve exactly 0.900 coverage across all models, as claimed in the theoretical results.

comments.title: Fixed-k procedure implementation unclear
comments.quote: We also report results from a conformalized fixed-k procedure, which finds the smallest fixed set size achieving coverage on the holdout set,  $k^{*}$ , then predicts sets of size  $k^{*} - 1$  or  $k^{*}$  on new examples in order to achieve exact coverage; see Algorithm 4 in Appendix E.
comments.explanation: The description of the fixed-k procedure contains a logical inconsistency. If $k^{*}$ is defined as the smallest fixed set size achieving coverage on the holdout set, then using sets of size $k^{*} - 1$ would necessarily fail to achieve coverage on that same holdout set by definition. The procedure cannot simultaneously guarantee coverage while sometimes using smaller sets than the minimum size needed for coverage. This suggests either the definition of $k^{*}$ is incorrect, or the procedure description is missing a crucial detail about how the randomization between $k^{*} - 1$ and $k^{*}$ preserves the coverage guarantee. Rewrite the description to clarify: 'finds the smallest fixed set size $k^{*}$ such that using sets of size $k^{*}$ achieves coverage, then randomizes between $k^{*} - 1$ and $k^{*}$ on new examples with appropriate probabilities to maintain exact marginal coverage.'

comments.title: Unsubstantiated coverage claim for distribution shift
comments.quote: The result shows that our method can still provide coverage even for models trained on different distributions, as long as the conformal calibration set comes from the new distribution.
comments.explanation: This claim about providing coverage for models trained on different distributions appears overstated given the experimental setup. The section states that the same procedure as Experiment 1 was repeated on ImageNet-V2, but doesn't specify whether the models were retrained on ImageNet-V2 or simply tested on it. If the models were trained on the original ImageNet distribution and only tested on ImageNet-V2 (which seems likely given the phrasing), then this doesn't demonstrate coverage for 'models trained on different distributions' - it only shows that the conformal prediction framework can handle distribution shift at test time when calibrated on the new distribution. The conformal prediction theory already guarantees this under exchangeability assumptions, so this isn't a novel finding about the method itself. Rewrite this sentence to clarify what was actually tested: either specify that models were retrained on ImageNet-V2, or rephrase to state that the method maintains coverage under test-time distribution shift when calibrated on the target distribution.

comments.title: Missing quantitative coverage results for ImageNet-V2
comments.quote: The result shows that our method can still provide coverage even for models trained on different distributions, as long as the conformal calibration set comes from the new distribution. The variance of the coverage is higher due to having less data.
comments.explanation: The section makes claims about coverage performance but provides no actual numerical results. Unlike Experiment 1 which presumably had quantitative coverage metrics, this section only states qualitatively that coverage is provided and variance is higher. For a results section, this is insufficient - readers need to see the actual coverage levels achieved, how they compare to the target coverage, and how much higher the variance is compared to Experiment 1. Without these numbers, it's impossible to evaluate whether the method truly 'provides coverage' or whether the increased variance makes the results practically unusable. Add the specific coverage percentages and variance measures for each model tested on ImageNet-V2, similar to how Experiment 1 likely reported its results.

comments.title: Table reference inconsistency
comments.quote: We now show that RAPS sets are smaller for easy images than hard ones, addressing the adaptiveness desideratum. Table 4 reports the size-stratified coverages of RAPS at the  $90\%$  level with  $k_{reg} = 5$  and different choices of  $\lambda$ .
comments.explanation: The text refers to 'Table 4' for size-stratified coverage results, but the actual table shown in this section is 'Table 2: Results on Imagenet-V2'. This creates a reference inconsistency that makes it impossible for readers to verify the claims about size-stratified coverage. The section should either include the correct Table 4 or update the reference to point to the appropriate table that contains the size-stratified coverage data.

comments.title: Proposition 3 proof missing
comments.quote: Proposition 3. Suppose  $P(Y \in \mathcal{C}(X) \mid X = x) = 1 - \alpha$  for each  $x \in \mathbb{R}^d$ . Then,  $P(Y \in \mathcal{C}(X) \mid \{|C(X)| \in \mathcal{A}\}) = 1 - \alpha$  for any  $\mathcal{A} \subset \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ .
comments.explanation: The proposition is mathematically correct but lacks a proof. The derivation follows from the law of total probability: $P(Y \in \mathcal{C}(X) \mid \{|C(X)| \in \mathcal{A}\}) = \sum_{x: |\mathcal{C}(x)| \in \mathcal{A}} P(Y \in \mathcal{C}(X) \mid X=x) P(X=x \mid \{|C(X)| \in \mathcal{A}\}) = (1-\alpha) \sum_{x: |\mathcal{C}(x)| \in \mathcal{A}} P(X=x \mid \{|C(X)| \in \mathcal{A}\}) = 1-\alpha$. Add this proof after the proposition statement.

comments.title: Overstated medical diagnosis claim
comments.quote: For classification tasks with many possible labels, our method enables a researcher to take any base classifier and return predictive sets guaranteed to achieve a pre-specified error level, such as  $90\%$ , while retaining small average size. It is simple to deploy, so it is an attractive, automatic way to quantify the uncertainty of image classifiers—an essential task in such settings as medical diagnostics, self-driving vehicles, and flagging dangerous internet content.
comments.explanation: The claim that RAPS provides 'guaranteed' error levels in medical diagnostics is overstated. The coverage guarantees in Theorem 1 and Proposition 1 rely critically on exchangeability between calibration and test data. In medical settings, distribution shift is common due to differences in patient populations, imaging equipment, or data collection protocols. The paper's own ImageNet-V2 experiment (Section 3.2) shows higher coverage variance under distribution shift, suggesting the guarantees may not hold in practice. The medical diagnosis application should be qualified to note that guarantees hold only when calibration data matches the deployment distribution.

comments.title: Confidence level definition inconsistency
comments.quote: or a pre-specified confidence level $\alpha$ such as 10%. Conformal predictors like our method can modify any black-box classifier to output predictive sets that are rigorously guaranteed to satisfy the desired *coverage* property shown in Eq. (1).
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly defines $α$ as a 'confidence level' when it is actually a significance level or error rate. In conformal prediction, $α$ represents the probability of error (the complement of coverage), so a 10% $α$ corresponds to 90% confidence/coverage. This creates confusion because the paper later refers to '95% prediction sets' in Figure 1, which would correspond to $α = 5%$, not $α = 10%$ as used in the example. Rewrite 'for a pre-specified confidence level $α$ such as 10%' as 'for a pre-specified significance level $α$ such as 10% (corresponding to 90% coverage)' to maintain consistency with standard conformal prediction terminology and the paper's own examples.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in calibration parameter
comments.quote: Then for  $\hat{\tau}_{\mathrm{cal}}$  defined as in Eq. (3), we have the following coverage guarantee:
comments.explanation: The theorem statement uses $\hat{\tau}_{\mathrm{cal}}$ while Equation (3) defines $\hat{\tau}_{\text{ccal}}$. These are clearly intended to be the same quantity since the theorem references Eq. (3). The inconsistency should be resolved by using $\hat{\tau}_{\text{ccal}}$ in the theorem statement to match the definition in Eq. (3).

comments.title: Undefined symbol in theorem statement
comments.quote: Suppose further that the sets  $\mathcal{C}(x,u,\tau)$  grow to include all labels for large enough  $\tau$ : for all  $x\in \mathbb{R}^d$ ,  $\mathcal{C}(x,u,\tau) = \mathcal{V}$  for some  $\tau$ .
comments.explanation: The symbol $\mathcal{V}$ appears without definition in the theorem statement. While context suggests it represents the full label space, this should be explicitly defined. The paper should add 'Let $\mathcal{V}$ denote the full label space' before the theorem statement.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 boundary case handling
comments.quote:
3: while  $\sum_{i = 1}^{L}s_{i} <   1 - \alpha$  do  $\triangleright$  Stop if  $1 - \alpha$  probability exceeded
4:  $L\gets L + 1$
comments.explanation: The algorithm doesn't explicitly handle the case where the sum of all scores is less than 1-α. If Σ_{i=1}^K s_i < 1-α (where K is the total number of classes), the while loop would continue incrementing L beyond K, potentially causing an out-of-bounds error. The algorithm should include a check: if L > K then return all classes. This boundary case is important for correctness when the classifier is poorly calibrated or when α is very small.

comments.title: Undefined sorted scores permutation
comments.quote: Input:  $\alpha$  sorted scores  $s$  associated permutation of classes  $I$  ,boolean rand
comments.explanation: The algorithm input mentions 'sorted scores s associated permutation of classes I' but doesn't specify the sorting order (ascending vs descending). This ambiguity affects the algorithm's behavior since the cumulative sum depends on the order. The paper should clarify that scores are sorted in descending order (highest probability first) to ensure the algorithm produces meaningful prediction sets that include the most likely classes first.

comments.title: Missing condition for upper bound claim
comments.quote:
In addition, for most families of set-valued functions $\mathcal{C}(x,u,\tau)$ there is a matching upper bound:

$P\Big{(}Y_{n+1}\in\mathcal{C}(X_{n+1},U_{n+1},\hat{\tau}_{\text{ccal}})\Big{)}\leq 1-\alpha+\frac{1}{n+1}.$

Roughly speaking, this will hold whenever the sets grow smoothly in $\tau$.
comments.explanation: The informal condition 'sets grow smoothly in $\tau$' is insufficient for a mathematical claim. The paper should either state the precise conditions from Lei et al. (2018) or remove the upper bound claim. As stated, the result is not verifiable without consulting external sources.

comments.title: Exchangeability condition insufficiently justified
comments.quote: As a technical remark, the theorem also holds if the observations to satisfy the weaker condition of exchangeability; see *Vovk et al. (2005)*.
comments.explanation: The paper claims the theorem holds under exchangeability but provides no justification. The proof in the i.i.d. case relies on symmetry arguments that extend to exchangeable sequences, but this connection should be briefly explained rather than deferred entirely to a citation.

comments.title: Undefined median-of-means procedure
comments.quote: Table 1: Results on Imagenet-Val. We report coverage and size of the optimal, randomized fixed sets, naive, APS, and RAPS sets for nine different Imagenet classifiers. The median-of-means for each column is reported over 100 different trials. See Section 3.1 for full details.
comments.explanation: The table mentions 'median-of-means' aggregation over 100 trials, but this procedure is not defined in the section or referenced to Section 3.1 (which is not provided in this excerpt). For conformal prediction methods, standard practice is to report single coverage values from one calibration/test split, not aggregated statistics over multiple trials. The median-of-means procedure needs to be defined: are these 100 different random splits of calibration/test data, or 100 different randomizations of the conformal procedure? Without this definition, the reported values cannot be interpreted correctly.

comments.title: Missing methodological details
comments.quote: We investigate the effect of regularization in more detail. For three values of  $\lambda$ , we collected the set sizes produced by each of naive, APS, and RAPS and report their histograms in Figure 4.
comments.explanation: The section text is too brief to properly evaluate the experimental setup. It mentions investigating regularization effects with three λ values but doesn't specify what those values are, how the calibration set was constructed, or what model was used. Since this is a results section, readers need the experimental parameters to interpret Figure 4. The text should state the specific λ values used (e.g., λ = 0.001, 0.01, 0.1) and confirm whether other parameters like k_reg were held constant during this experiment.

comments.title: Table 4 APS data missing
comments.quote: In Table 4, we report on the coverage of APS and RAPS, stratified by the size of the prediction set. Turning our attention to the  $\lambda = 0$  column, we see that when APS outputs a set of size  $101 - 1000$ , APS has coverage  $97\%$ , substantially higher than  $90\%$  nominal rate.
comments.explanation: The text states Table 4 reports coverage for both APS and RAPS, but the table only shows columns labeled with λ values. Since λ=0 corresponds to APS, the table header should explicitly label this column as 'APS (λ=0)' to avoid confusion. Currently, readers cannot distinguish APS results from RAPS with λ=0. Rewrite the table header to show 'APS (λ=0)' instead of just 'λ = 0'.

comments.title: Table 4 coverage calculation unclear
comments.quote: Table 4: Coverage conditional on set size. We report average coverage of images stratified by the size of the set output by RAPS using a ResNet-152 for varying  $\lambda$ . The marginal coverage rate is  $90\%$ .
comments.explanation: The table shows 'cnt' (count) and 'cvg' (coverage) columns but doesn't explain how these combine to yield the 90% marginal coverage. The calculation should be: $\sum_{\text{bins}} \frac{\text{cnt}_i}{\text{total count}} \times \text{cvg}_i = 0.90$. Add this formula and show one example calculation to verify the 90% marginal coverage claim.

comments.title: Oracle procedure assumption needs justification
comments.quote: Given such a model, the oracle procedure from Romano et al. (2020) would return the correct label with probability  $1 - \alpha$  and the empty set with probability  $\alpha$ .
comments.explanation: This claim assumes a perfect classifier with infinite data, but the connection between 'high accuracy' and 'perfect determination of Y by X' is not established. The paper should clarify that this oracle behavior requires $P(Y=f(X)|X) = 1$ for some deterministic function f, which is stronger than just high accuracy. Add 'when Y is deterministically determined by X' after 'high-signal problems' to make this assumption explicit.

comments.title: Cascade efficiency claim unsupported
comments.quote: In a different direction, one can improve efficiency of a classifier by using a cheap classifier outputting a prediction set first, and an expensive one only when the cheap classifier outputs a large set (a cascade; see, e.g., Li et al. 2015), and Fisch et al. (2021) for an implementation of conformal prediction in this setting.
comments.explanation: The discussion suggests RAPS could be used in cascade systems but provides no evidence that conformal prediction sets are suitable for this purpose. The referenced Fisch et al. (2021) work is mentioned but not explained - it's unclear whether that work uses conformal prediction in cascades or addresses different challenges. For cascades to work effectively, the prediction sets need to be calibrated to trigger the expensive classifier at appropriate rates, but the paper doesn't analyze this trade-off or provide any empirical validation of cascade performance using RAPS.

comments.title: Medical screening example lacks feasibility analysis
comments.quote: Prediction sets are most useful for problems with many classes; returning to our initial medical motivation, we envision RAPS could be used by a doctor to automatically screen for a large number of diseases (e.g. via a blood sample) and refer the patient to relevant specialists.
comments.explanation: The medical screening example is speculative and ignores practical constraints. Screening for 'a large number of diseases' via blood samples would require a classifier trained on multi-label data where patients can have multiple diseases simultaneously, but RAPS is designed for single-label classification as stated in the problem setup. Additionally, the paper doesn't address how calibration data would be collected for rare diseases or how the method would handle the severe class imbalance typical in medical screening applications.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes Regularized Adaptive Prediction Sets (RAPS), a method to produce conformal prediction sets for image classifiers. By incorporating a regularization term into the conformal scoring function, the authors aim to reduce the size of prediction sets while maintaining rigorous marginal coverage guarantees. The method is evaluated on ImageNet and ImageNet-V2, showing improved efficiency compared to standard Adaptive Prediction Sets (APS).

The paper provides a clean, practical, and theoretically grounded contribution to uncertainty quantification in computer vision. It successfully bridges the gap between abstract conformal prediction theory and the realities of modern deep learning, offering a clear improvement over existing baselines. The empirical evaluation is extensive and the code release adds significant value to the community.

Overview issues:

- Sensitivity to Calibration Set Representativeness: The validity of the conformal guarantee in Theorem 1 relies on the assumption that the calibration set and the test data are exchangeable. While the authors acknowledge this, the practical performance of RAPS is highly sensitive to the distribution of the calibration set, especially under distribution shift. In Section 3.2, the authors show results on ImageNet-V2, but the paper lacks a systematic analysis of how the size and composition of the calibration set (e.g., class imbalance or domain-specific bias) affect the stability of the coverage guarantee. To strengthen the paper, the authors should include a sensitivity analysis demonstrating the robustness of the coverage guarantee when the calibration set is systematically biased or smaller than the 20K samples used in the primary experiments.

- Lack of Discussion on Computational Overhead: The abstract claims the method is 'fast like Platt scaling', yet the paper does not provide a quantitative comparison of the inference-time computational overhead. While the calculation of the conformal score is straightforward, the need to sort probabilities and compute the regularization term for every test-time image introduces a non-negligible cost, particularly for models with a large number of classes (like ImageNet). The authors should provide a table or figure comparing the latency of standard softmax prediction against the RAPS procedure to justify the claim of practical efficiency in real-world deployment scenarios.

- Ambiguity in Hyperparameter Selection Strategy: The performance of RAPS is governed by the hyperparameters lambda and k_reg, as shown in Equation 4. While the authors mention in Appendix E that these parameters can be selected from data, the main text does not sufficiently detail the criteria for this selection. If the choice of these parameters is optimized to minimize set size on a validation set, this could potentially lead to overfitting the calibration step, thereby compromising the coverage guarantee. The authors need to explicitly clarify whether the parameters are tuned on a separate holdout set to avoid data leakage and provide a more rigorous justification for the chosen heuristic for k_reg.

- Conditional Coverage and Class Imbalance: The authors discuss in Section 4 that conditional coverage is difficult to achieve, but they do not adequately address how class imbalance in the underlying dataset affects the conditional coverage properties of RAPS. If a specific class is rare, the model might consistently produce larger sets for that class, leading to disparate performance across categories. The paper would benefit from a brief analysis of per-class coverage rates to ensure that the marginal coverage guarantee does not mask poor performance on underrepresented classes, which is a critical concern in medical or safety-critical applications.

- Lack of empirical validation for the proposed parameter selection heuristic: The authors suggest that the hyperparameters lambda and k_reg can be selected from data, but the paper lacks a systematic simulation or empirical demonstration of the stability of this selection process. Specifically, it is unclear if the proposed heuristic for selecting these parameters on a calibration set leads to significant variance in set size or coverage across different random splits. To address this, the authors should provide a simulation study using, for example, a ResNet-50 on ImageNet, showing the distribution of the final set sizes when hyperparameters are chosen via the proposed heuristic versus a grid search on a separate holdout set.

- Absence of a formal comparison to existing non-conformal uncertainty methods: While the paper positions itself against naive and APS methods, it fails to provide a formal comparison against state-of-the-art non-conformal uncertainty quantification methods, such as Deep Ensembles or Monte Carlo Dropout. These methods are commonly used in practice, and readers need to understand the trade-offs in terms of set size and computational cost versus the formal coverage guarantees provided by RAPS. A comparative table reporting the average set size and coverage for a ResNet-152 using RAPS versus a top-performing ensemble method would clarify the practical value of the proposed approach.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a quantitative latency analysis comparing standard inference with the RAPS procedure.

- Provide a clearer explanation or a formal procedure for the selection of hyperparameters lambda and k_reg to ensure no data leakage occurs.

- Add a brief analysis of per-class coverage to address potential issues with class imbalance.

comments.title: Inconsistency in RAPS performance claims
comments.quote: For example, using a ResNeXt-101, naive does not achieve coverage, while APS and RAPS achieve it almost exactly. However, APS sets have an average size of 19, while RAPS sets have an average size of 2 at  $\alpha = 10\%$  (Figure 2 and Table
comments.explanation: The claim that RAPS achieves an average set size of 2 while APS achieves 19 for ResNeXt-101 at α=0.1 is highly implausible for ImageNet, which has 1,000 classes. If the model is reasonably accurate, the top-1 accuracy for ResNeXt-101 is typically high enough that the set size for a 90% coverage guarantee should be much smaller than 19 for both methods. An average set size of 19 implies that the model is essentially guessing among 2% of the total classes, which contradicts the known performance of ResNeXt-101. Please verify if the reported average set sizes in Table 1 are correct or if they were mislabeled in the text.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of ensemble training requirements
comments.quote: These methods also have major practical limitations; for example, ensembling requires training many copies of a neural network adversarially.
comments.explanation: The statement that ensembling requires training models 'adversarially' is factually incorrect. Standard deep ensembles, such as those proposed by Lakshminarayanan et al. (2017), involve training multiple independent models with different random initializations or data subsets. While adversarial training is a technique used for robustness, it is not a requirement for standard ensemble-based uncertainty quantification. This mischaracterization overstates the difficulty of implementing ensemble methods.

comments.title: Inconsistency in fixed-k procedure description
